attributes.description: Alerts when the average HTTP request time on a CouchDB node exceeds a configurable latency threshold. Rising request times indicate disk I/O saturation, lock contention, view rebuilds, or general resource pressure.
attributes.name: [CouchDB OTel] High average request time (latency)

investigation_guide.blob:
## High Average Request Time (Latency)

### Triage Steps
1. Open the CouchDB overview dashboard and identify the node and time range where latency spiked.
2. Correlate with HTTP request rate — was there a traffic surge, or did latency climb at constant load?
3. Check `couchdb.database.operations` (reads vs writes) and `couchdb.httpd.bulk_requests` for workload composition shifts.
4. Review host metrics: disk I/O wait, CPU saturation, and memory pressure.
5. Check for view rebuilds — design document changes trigger costly background indexing that can stall queries.

### Remediation
- If view rebuilds are the cause, schedule design document changes during off-peak hours and pre-warm views with `?stale=update_after`.
- For disk I/O saturation, move data files to faster storage or reduce write rate via batching.
- Tune `[httpd] max_connections` and `[chttpd] backlog` to prevent request queueing under load.
- Consider compacting databases (`POST /{db}/_compact`) if file sizes are bloated.

### Threshold Tuning
The default 1000ms threshold is a generic starting point. Set it to align with your CouchDB SLO — typical OLTP workloads target <100-500ms. The rule uses peak (MAX) latency in the window, so brief spikes are detected; switch to averaging if you want to filter out transient outliers.

esqlQuery.esql:
TS metrics-couchdbreceiver.otel-default
// Average request time is a gauge — use MAX_OVER_TIME for peak detection
| WHERE couchdb.average_request_time IS NOT NULL
| STATS max_avg_request_time_ms = MAX(MAX_OVER_TIME(couchdb.average_request_time))
  BY couchdb.node.name
// Latency threshold in ms — adjust for your SLO (e.g. 500–2000 ms)
| WHERE max_avg_request_time_ms > 1000
| SORT max_avg_request_time_ms DESC
| LIMIT 20
